text:
The 1% rule will lie to you if you stop there.

Here's the shortcut: monthly rent should be about 1% of purchase price. A $200K property should rent for roughly $2,000/month. It's a fast filter, not a final answer.

What it misses: property taxes, insurance, vacancy, maintenance, and management. A property can pass the 1% rule and still lose money once you run the real cash flow.

Use it to screen deals fast. Then run the actual numbers before you get attached.

Save this before your next walkthrough.

visual.openai_prompt: Create a premium real estate deal-room visual for The Real Estate Deal Lab. The image should feel premium, practical, and grounded, not flashy.
visual.headline: THE 1% RULE ISN'T THE WHOLE STORY
visual.subtext: Screen fast. Verify with real numbers.

text:
ARV is a guess until you prove it.

Too many first-time investors anchor to a rehab budget before they've locked the after-repair value. That's backwards. Here's the order that protects your capital:

1. Pull 3-5 true comps (same condition class, sold in the last 6 months, similar square footage).
2. Set ARV from the comps, not from hope.
3. Scope the rehab against that ARV, not the other way around.
4. Apply the 70% rule: Max offer = (ARV x 0.70) - repair costs.
5. If the number doesn't work, the deal doesn't work yet. Renegotiate or walk.

This is the exact sequence we run every property through in the Deal Analyzer.

Try the Deal Analyzer.

text:
I've sat across the table from more bad deals than good ones.

That's not a knock on the investors who brought them. Most of them just never had someone check the math before they got emotionally attached to the property.

A good deal should survive the math, not just the walkthrough. That's the entire reason the Lab exists: a place to bring a property before you make an offer, not after you're already stuck in one.

If you want a second set of eyes on your numbers, that's exactly what we do inside the Lab.

Join the Lab.

text:
Before you call an agent, check this.

Quick poll: when you look at a listing, what do you check first?
A) The rent estimate
B) The rehab cost
C) The neighborhood
D) Honestly, none of it yet

Most deals fall apart because the order was wrong, not because the property was bad. Grab the free guide and run your next listing through the right sequence before you pick up the phone.
